category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Linear Algebra/Systems of Linear Equations

info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Systems of Linear Equations

questiontext.text: The system \(Ax = b\) (with \(A\) an \(m\times n\) matrix) is consistent (has at least one solution) if and only if:

answer.text: \(\text{rank}(A) = \text{rank}([A \mid b])\) (the augmented matrix has the same rank as \(A\))

feedback.text: Correct. By the Rouché-Capelli theorem, \(Ax=b\) is consistent iff \(\text{rank}(A)=\text{rank}(A|b)\), i.e., \(b\) is in the column space of \(A\).
Incorrect. Consistency requires \(b\in\text{col}(A)\); invertibility is sufficient but not necessary (rectangular systems can be consistent).
Incorrect. Full column rank ensures at most one solution, not existence.
Incorrect. Full row rank guarantees consistency for every \(b\), but consistency for a specific \(b\) requires only \(\text{rank}(A)=\text{rank}(A|b)\).

questiontext.text: If \(x_p\) is a particular solution of \(Ax=b\) and \(x_h\) is the general solution of \(Ax=0\), then the general solution of \(Ax=b\) is:

feedback.text: Correct. The complete solution is a particular solution plus the null space of \(A\): \(\{x_p + x_h : x_h\in\ker A\}\).
Incorrect. Solutions are added (affine translation), not multiplied.

Incorrect. Since \(x_h\) represents all homogeneous solutions (which form a subspace closed under negation), this is equivalent to \(x_p+x_h\)—but subtracting doesn't change the set, just the representation.
Incorrect. The particular solution is essential; without it, we only have solutions to \(Ax=0\), not \(Ax=b\).

questiontext.text: The system \(Ax=b\) (\(A\) is \(n\times n\)) has a unique solution for every \(b\in\mathbb{R}^n\) if and only if:

feedback.text: Correct. A square system has a unique solution for every \(b\) iff \(A\) is invertible: then \(x=A^{-1}b\).
Incorrect. Symmetry does not guarantee invertibility.
Incorrect. Nonzero trace does not ensure invertibility.
Incorrect. The uniqueness of the solution depends on the matrix \(A\), not on \(b\).

questiontext.text: The augmented matrix \([A\mid b] = \begin{pmatrix}1&2&3&|&1\\0&1&2&|&2\\0&0&0&|&1\end{pmatrix}\). The system is:

feedback.text: Correct. The last row gives \(0=1\), a contradiction. So \(\text{rank}(A)=2\) but \(\text{rank}([A|b])=3\); the system is inconsistent.
Incorrect. The equation \(0x_1+0x_2+0x_3=1\) is impossible; there is no solution.
Incorrect. The last row \(0=1\) is a contradiction, making the system inconsistent.
Incorrect. Linear systems have 0, 1, or infinitely many solutions. The system here has 0 solutions.

questiontext.text: A consistent system \(Ax=b\) with \(A\) being \(m\times n\) of rank \(r\) has how many free variables?

feedback.text: Correct. By rank-nullity: the number of free variables equals the nullity = \(n - \text{rank}(A) = n-r\).
Incorrect. Free variables count is based on columns minus rank: \(n-r\), not \(m-r\).
Incorrect. There are \(r\) pivot variables and \(n-r\) free variables.
Incorrect. There are \(n-r\) free variables; only when \(r=0\) are all \(n\) variables free.

questiontext.text: The homogeneous system \(Ax=0\) with \(A\) an \(m\times n\) matrix always has a nontrivial solution when:

feedback.text: Correct. If \(n>m\), then \(\text{rank}(A)\leq m < n\), so nullity \(= n-\text{rank}(A)\geq n-m>0\). There is always a nontrivial solution.
Incorrect. When \(n=m\), \(Ax=0\) has only the trivial solution iff \(A\) is invertible. Nontrivial solutions exist only when \(\det(A)=0\).
Incorrect. When \(n<m\), \(\text{rank}(A)\leq n\), and if \(\text{rank}(A)=n\), the only solution is trivial.
Incorrect. With one equation and \(n\geq2\) unknowns there are nontrivial solutions, but the condition is \(n>m\) in general.

questiontext.text: When \(Ax=b\) is inconsistent, the least squares solution minimizes \(\|Ax-b\|^2\). It satisfies the normal equations:

feedback.text: Correct. Minimizing \(\|Ax-b\|^2\) gives the normal equations \(A^TAx=A^Tb\). The solution is \(\hat{x}=(A^TA)^{-1}A^Tb\) when \(A\) has full column rank.
Incorrect. The normal equations for least squares are \(A^TAx=A^Tb\).
Incorrect. The original system \(Ax=b\) is inconsistent, so we solve the normal equations \(A^TAx=A^Tb\) instead.
Incorrect. The normal equations are \(A^TA\hat{x}=A^Tb\).

questiontext.text: An overdetermined system (\(m > n\)) \(Ax = b\) (full column rank \(A\)):

answer.text: Has at most one solution (unique if consistent); the least squares solution is unique and given by \(\hat{x}=(A^TA)^{-1}A^Tb\)

feedback.text: Correct. Full column rank implies \(A^TA\) is invertible, giving a unique least squares solution. The system itself may be inconsistent.
Incorrect. Full column rank means the null space is trivial; at most one solution exists.
Incorrect. Overdetermined systems are generically inconsistent.

answer.text: Has no solution and no best approximation

feedback.text: Incorrect. Even if inconsistent, the least squares solution provides the best approximation.

questiontext.text: The system \(x_1 + x_2 + x_3 = 1\) and \(x_1 - x_2 = 0\) has general solution (setting \(x_3 = t\) free):

feedback.text: Incorrect. With 3 unknowns and 2 equations (rank 2), there is one free variable, giving infinitely many solutions.
Incorrect. This satisfies \(x_1+x_2+x_3=t+(1-t)+0=1\)✓ but not \(x_1-x_2=t-(1-t)=2t-1\neq0\) generally.
Incorrect. This is one particular solution, not the general solution.

questiontext.text: Gaussian elimination on an \(n\times n\) system requires approximately how many arithmetic operations?

feedback.text: Correct. Gaussian elimination requires \(\frac{2}{3}n^3 + O(n^2)\) operations, i.e., \(O(n^3)\) complexity.
Incorrect. \(O(n^2)\) is the complexity of matrix-vector multiplication, not full elimination.
Incorrect. \(O(n)\) operations would solve the system in linear time, which is not achievable for general dense systems.
Incorrect. Gaussian elimination is polynomial (\(O(n^3)\)), not exponential.

questiontext.text: An underdetermined consistent system \(Ax=b\) (\(m < n\), \(\text{rank}(A)=m\)) has:

answer.text: Infinitely many solutions; the minimum-norm solution is \(\hat{x} = A^T(AA^T)^{-1}b\)

feedback.text: Correct. Underdetermined consistent systems (full row rank) have infinitely many solutions. The minimum-norm solution uses the right pseudoinverse \(A^+=A^T(AA^T)^{-1}\).
Incorrect. Underdetermined consistent systems have infinitely many solutions (a whole affine subspace).
Incorrect. With full row rank, \(b\in\text{col}(A)\) for any \(b\), so the system is always consistent.
Incorrect. The homogeneous underdetermined system (\(b=0\)) has infinitely many solutions (nontrivial null space).

questiontext.text: For \(A = \begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix}\), which vector \(b\) makes \(Ax=b\) consistent?

feedback.text: Correct. We need \(b\in\text{col}(A)\), i.e., \(b_3=b_1+b_2\). Check: \(3=1+2\)✓. Solution: \(x=(1,2)^T\).
Incorrect. Consistency requires \(b_3=b_1+b_2=1+2=3\neq4\). This system is inconsistent.
Incorrect. \(b_1+b_2=0+0=0\neq1=b_3\). This system is inconsistent.
Incorrect. \(b_1+b_2=1+1=2\neq3=b_3\). This system is inconsistent.

questiontext.text: The Gauss-Seidel iterative method for solving \(Ax=b\) converges if:

answer.text: \(A\) is strictly diagonally dominant (each diagonal entry exceeds the sum of absolute values of off-diagonal entries in its row)

feedback.text: Correct. Strict diagonal dominance is a sufficient condition for convergence of both Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel iterations.
Incorrect. If \(A\) is singular, \(Ax=b\) either has no solution or infinitely many, and iterative methods would not converge to a unique solution.
Incorrect. Positive trace is not a convergence criterion. Gauss-Seidel converges for symmetric positive definite \(A\), not just positive trace.

answer.text: The spectral radius of \(A\) is less than 1

feedback.text: Incorrect. The spectral radius condition applies to the iteration matrix \(M^{-1}N\) (from the splitting \(A=M-N\)), not to \(A\) itself.

questiontext.text: For a \(4\times6\) matrix \(A\) with \(\text{rank}(A)=3\), the system \(Ax=b\) (when consistent) has a general solution with how many free parameters?

feedback.text: Correct. Nullity \(= n - \text{rank} = 6-3=3\). The solution space is a particular solution plus a 3-dimensional null space.
Incorrect. Nullity \(= 6-3=3\), giving 3 free parameters.
Incorrect. The number of free parameters equals the nullity \(= n-r = 6-3=3\).
Incorrect. There are 3 pivot variables and \(6-3=3\) free variables.

questiontext.text: The system of linear equations \(2x + 4y = 6\) and \(3x + 6y = 9\) has:

answer.text: Infinitely many solutions: \(x = 3 - 2t,\; y = t\) for any \(t\in\mathbb{R}\)

feedback.text: Correct. Both equations reduce to \(x+2y=3\). Free parameter \(y=t\) gives \(x=3-2t\). The system has rank 1 (equations are proportional).
Incorrect. Both equations are equivalent (\(3\times\) eq.1 = \(2\times\) eq.2), so there are infinitely many solutions.
Incorrect. With rank 1 and 2 unknowns, there is one free variable, giving infinitely many solutions.
Incorrect. Linear systems cannot have exactly 2 solutions; they have 0, 1, or infinitely many.

questiontext.text: The condition number \(\kappa(A) = \|A\|\|A^{-1}\|\) of a matrix measures sensitivity of \(Ax=b\) to perturbations. If \(\kappa(A)\) is large, then:

answer.text: Small relative changes in \(b\) can cause large relative changes in the solution \(x\) (ill-conditioned system)

feedback.text: Correct. The bound \(\frac{\|\delta x\|}{\|x\|}\leq\kappa(A)\frac{\|\delta b\|}{\|b\|}\) shows that a large condition number amplifies relative errors.

answer.text: The system always has no solution

feedback.text: Incorrect. A large condition number means the system is ill-conditioned but still has a unique solution (if \(A\) is invertible).
Incorrect. \(\kappa(A)=\infty\) for singular matrices, but a large finite \(\kappa\) means nearly-singular (ill-conditioned), not exactly singular.

answer.text: The system cannot be solved numerically

feedback.text: Incorrect. Ill-conditioned systems can still be solved numerically, but solutions may have large numerical errors.

questiontext.text: Over the field \(\mathbb{F}_2 = \{0,1\}\) (arithmetic mod 2), the system \(\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix}\) has solution:

feedback.text: Correct. From row 2: \(x=1\). From row 1: \(1+y=1\pmod{2}\), so \(y=0\). Verify: \((1+0,1+0)=(1,1)\)✓ mod 2.
Incorrect. Row 2: \(x+0\cdot y=x=1\neq0\pmod{2}\).
Incorrect. Row 2: \(x=1\)✓. Row 1: \(1+1=0\neq1\pmod{2}\). So this fails.
Incorrect. The matrix has \(\det=0\cdot1-1\cdot1=-1=1\pmod{2}\neq0\), so it is invertible and the system has a unique solution.

questiontext.text: For what value(s) of \(\lambda\) does the system \(x+y=1\), \(2x+\lambda y=2\) have infinitely many solutions?

feedback.text: Correct. \(\det\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\2&\lambda\end{pmatrix}=\lambda-2=0\) gives \(\lambda=2\). When \(\lambda=2\), equation 2 becomes \(2(x+y)=2\), identical to equation 1, giving infinitely many solutions.
Incorrect. When \(\lambda=0\), \(\det=0-2=-2\neq0\), so the system has a unique solution.
Incorrect. When \(\lambda=1\), \(\det=1-2=-1\neq0\), giving a unique solution.
Incorrect. Only \(\lambda=2\) makes the determinant zero, yielding infinitely many solutions.

questiontext.text: A system of \(m\) equations in \(n\) unknowns over \(\mathbb{R}\) with coefficient matrix of rank \(r\). The number of linearly independent equations is:

feedback.text: Correct. The rank equals the number of linearly independent rows (equations), which is the dimension of the row space.
Incorrect. If some equations are linear combinations of others, the number of independent equations is less than \(m\).
Incorrect. The number of independent equations is the rank \(r\leq\min(m,n)\), not necessarily \(n\).
Incorrect. The number of independent equations is the rank \(r\), not \(m-n\).

questiontext.text: Let \(S\) be the solution set of \(Ax=b\) (nonempty) and \(S_0\) be the solution set of \(Ax=0\). Which is true?

answer.text: \(S\) is an affine subspace (coset of \(S_0\)): \(S = x_p + S_0\) for any particular solution \(x_p\)

feedback.text: Correct. The solution set of a consistent system is a coset (affine subspace) of the null space: \(S=x_p+\ker A\).

answer.text: \(S\) is a vector subspace (closed under addition and scalar multiplication)

feedback.text: Incorrect. \(S\) is a vector subspace only when \(b=0\). For \(b\neq0\), \(0\notin S\), so \(S\) is not a subspace.
Incorrect. If \(b=0\), then \(S=S_0\). For \(b\neq0\), they are disjoint, but the claim "always" is wrong.
Incorrect. \(S\) and \(S_0\) have the same cardinality (both infinite or both trivial).
